narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and I know receiving this rejection must feel incredibly disheartening amid the unique challenges you may have faced. Your profile stands out with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical student's 3.21—and an LSAT score of 36, close to the average of 36.70, showing real academic promise from your prior studies. You're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for most applicants at 92.7%, and your family's middle-income bracket aligns with many others in the dataset. However, your Year 1 school decile of 2 is notably lower than the average of 5.71, and your Year 3 decile of 1 falls well below the typical 5.53, reflecting some tougher moments in your grades during law school. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated your chance of failing at 41%, which crossed the threshold leading to this rejection—essentially, it flagged a higher risk of not passing on the first try compared to profiles it deems safer bets for approval. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile rank, as it suggested challenges in maintaining strong performance late in your program, far below what the model sees in successful bar takers. On a brighter note, your LSAT score strongly pulled in the opposite direction, arguing convincingly for your potential to pass, while your undergraduate GPA provided similar positive support, highlighting your foundational strengths. Your lower Year 1 decile added a modest nudge toward the failure side, but your full-time status and family income each offered small boosts toward success. Compared to typical students who pass, your later-year grades diverged most sharply downward, overshadowing your other solid metrics and tipping the scales. While this stings, especially as a Black woman navigating systemic barriers in legal education, it's not the end—consider retaking courses or targeted bar prep to boost those deciles, perhaps seeking mentorship programs tailored for women of color in law, and reapply with strengthened evidence of your resilience. You've already shown impressive undergrad success; building on that could shift future predictions in your favor. Keep pushing—you belong in this field.
